1: wayyāḇōʾ yôsēp wayyaggēḏ lĕparʿōh — Joseph comes and tells Pharaoh; the sequential waw-consecutives narrate Joseph's court appearance. The report to Pharaoh uses the same cattle-inventory formula as Joseph's coaching (46:32): "flocks and herds and everything they have" (ṣōʾnām ûḇĕqārām wĕḵol-ʾăšer lāhem). The verbal parallel between the coaching (46:32) and the report (47:1) shows the brothers followed Joseph's instruction exactly — a small narrative confirmation that the plan was executed as planned.
2: ûmiqqĕṣēh ʾeḥāyw — "from the extremity/end of his brothers" — the five selected are chosen from the extremity (qāṣeh) of the brothers. The phrase is ambiguous: "from the extremes" could mean the oldest and youngest, or the least distinguished, or a representative sampling. The LXX adds "five men," consistent with the MT. The selection of five (half of ten brothers) for the Pharaonic presentation was probably a practical judgment — presenting all ten at once would be unwieldy, while five represents the family adequately.
3: rōʿê ṣōʾn ʿăḇādêkā — "shepherds of flocks are your servants" — the brothers' self-designation as "your servants" (ʿăḇādêkā) is the standard court-protocol of address to the king; it implies subordination and request. "Just as our fathers were (gam-ʾăḇōtênû)" — the "also our fathers" adds the hereditary dimension: this is not a new trade adopted under pressure but a generational identity. The echo of Joseph's coaching (46:34: "your servants have tended livestock from our boyhood, just as our fathers did") shows the brothers followed the prepared script faithfully.
4: lāgûr bāʾāreṣ bāʾnû — "we have come to sojourn (gûr) in the land." The verb gûr (to sojourn as a resident alien, without full land rights) is the key term of the patriarchal gēr identity (Abram as gēr, 12:10; 17:8; 20:1; Isaac at Gerar, 26:3; the Egyptian oppression described as Israel's gēr status, Deut 26:5). The brothers' self-identification as gērîm before Pharaoh is simultaneously a legal request (give us sojourner status) and a narrative echo of the entire patriarchal covenant-journey. kî-kāḇēḏ hārāʿāḇ — "because the famine is severe" — the causal clause justifies the sojourn request.
5: ʾaḇîkā wĕʾaḥêkā bāʾû ʾēlêkā — Pharaoh's acknowledgment: "your father and your brothers have come to you" — the possessive forms (ʾaḇîkā, ʾaḥêkā, your father, your brothers) frame the family as Joseph's family in Pharaoh's speech; Pharaoh recognizes the family through Joseph. The grammatical point: Pharaoh speaks of the family as Joseph's (ʾēlêkā, to you), not as subjects of his own — the covenant-family retains its own identity even in Egypt under Pharaoh's patronage.
6: ʾereṣ miṣrayim lĕpānêkā hîʾ — "the land of Egypt is before you" — the same "before you" (lĕpānêkā) formula of ch13 (YHWH to Abram: "all the land that you see I will give to you"). Pharaoh mirrors the covenant-formula without knowing it; the "best of the land" (bĕmêṭaḇ hāʾāreṣ) offered to Jacob's family echoes the "best part of the land" that Joseph gave them (v11). wĕyēšĕḇû bĕʾereṣ gōšen — the settlement in Goshen fulfills Joseph's strategy (46:34: the shepherd-abomination ensures Goshen placement).
7: waybāreḵ yaʿăqōḇ ʾet-parʿōh — "Jacob blessed Pharaoh." The verb bārak (Piel: to bless) with Jacob as subject and Pharaoh as object is the narrative's most politically charged moment. In ANE convention, a person with superior access to divine favor could bless one of lesser standing; Jacob, as the covenant-patriarch, carries YHWH's blessing-capacity established in 12:2-3. The blessing of Pharaoh by the immigrant patriarch echoes the covenant's promise that "all nations will be blessed through you" (12:3) — beginning with the world's most powerful king.
8: kamāh yĕmê šĕnê ḥayyêkā — "How many are the days of the years of your life?" Pharaoh's question uses the triple accumulation — days (yāmîm) + years (šānîm) + life (ḥayyîm) — which is the most expansive formula for a person's age. The question is not merely polite inquiry but a recognition of Jacob's evident antiquity; the old patriarch arriving from Canaan presented an image of extreme age even in a culture that respected age highly.
9: šĕnê mĕgûrāy — "the years of my sojourning/pilgrimage." The noun mĕgûr (from gûr, to sojourn) means the period of one's sojourning as an alien — it is both a temporal and a status word. Jacob's self-description as "sojourning" (not "living" or "dwelling") defines his entire life as a pilgrimage without a permanent home. mĕʿaṭ wĕrāʿîm — "few and evil/difficult." Rāʿ (bad/evil/distressing) applied to Jacob's years is the same adjective used for the "bad years" of famine (41:27). The contrast with "the pilgrimage of my fathers" (mĕgûrê ʾăḇōtāy) acknowledges Abraham (175) and Isaac (180) as models of longer covenant-lives.
10: waybāreḵ yaʿăqōḇ ʾet-parʿōh — Jacob blesses Pharaoh a second time upon leaving (forming a bracket: he blessed Pharaoh upon arrival, v7, and again upon departure, v10). The double blessing is the liturgical frame of the encounter: the blessing is not merely a convention but the covenant-patriarch's formal transmission of divine favor to the pagan ruler. The departure-blessing is distinct from the arrival-blessing — the arrival-blessing is a greeting; the departure-blessing is a benediction, a sending-away with divine benefit.
11: bĕʾereṣ rameśēs — "in the land of Rameses." This anachronistic place-name (the city Rameses was built by Israelite labor in Exod 1:11) uses the later name known to the audience rather than the Bronze Age name of the Goshen-district. The geographic identification locates Goshen within the territory of the later Rameses administrative district in the eastern Nile delta — consistent with archaeological evidence for Israelite presence in the eastern delta during the New Kingdom period.
12: lĕpî haṭṭap — "according to the number of their children/little ones" — ṭap refers specifically to the youngest children in a household; the phrase "by the mouth of the little ones" means food was apportioned based on how many children each family unit had to feed. The attention to children in the provision-formula reflects the covenant-people's demographic priority — the multiplication of Jacob's family in Egypt (47:27: "they were fruitful and increased greatly") requires adequate provision for the youngest generation.
13: lēḥem ʾên bĕḵol-hāʾāreṣ — "there was no bread/food in all the land" — the starkness of the famine is restated after the settlement of Jacob's family; the narrative refocuses on the larger economic crisis that Joseph must manage. The conjunction kî-kāḇēḏ hārāʿāḇ mĕʾōḏ — "because the famine was very severe" — uses the same kāḇēḏ (heavy) vocabulary applied earlier to the famine (41:31: "very severe") and to Pharaoh's hardened heart (Exod 7:14: "Pharaoh's heart is hardened").
14: wayyiqabbēṣ yôsēp ʾet-kol-hakkesep — Joseph "gathered/collected" (qibbēṣ, Piel intensive) all the money. The Piel of qāḇaṣ is the intensive collecting-together form — a comprehensive, thorough gathering rather than a casual accumulation. wayyāḇēʾ ʾet-hakkesep bêtāh parʿōh — "he brought the silver to Pharaoh's house/palace." The systematic centralization of all monetary wealth in Egypt and Canaan to Pharaoh's treasury through the grain-sale is the economic dimension of Joseph's administration.
15: lāmmāh nāmût negdekā — "why should we die before your eyes (nĕgdĕkā)?" — the appeal "before your eyes" is the covenant-loyalty formula inverted: the Egyptians appeal to Joseph's presence and authority as their reason not to die. kî ʾāpas hakkāsep — "because the silver has run out" (ʾāpas, to come to an end/be exhausted — a poetic and strong verb for the complete depletion of resources). The transition from money-purchase to barter is the narrative's marker of economic desperation deepening.
16: hāḇû miqnêkem — "give/bring your livestock" — the imperative hāḇû (from yāḇ or nātan: give!) is a strong command-form. The exchange structure shifts: first currency (v14), then livestock (vv16-17), then land and persons (vv19-20). The progressive stages of asset-transfer chart the famine's systematic economic devastation of Egypt. ûʾettnāh lākem bĕmiqnêkem — "and I will give to you for your livestock" — the exchange principle remains consistent: value given for value received.
17: bassûsîm ûḇĕmiqnēh haṣṣōʾn ûḇĕmiqnēh habbāqār — "horses, sheep and goats, cattle" — the inventory of livestock exchanged for grain lists the primary categories of ANE pastoral wealth: horses (prestige animals, also military assets), sheep/goats (flock animals, the most common pastoral wealth), cattle (draft and meat animals). waynĕhălem baśśānāh hahîʾ — "he brought them through (nāhal, to guide/lead) that year" — the Piel of nāhal is the shepherd's leading-to-water idiom (Ps 23:2: "he leads me beside still waters"), here applied to Joseph's economic leadership of Egypt through the famine year.
18: lōʾ-nĕkahēḏ mēʾăḏōnî — "we cannot hide (kāḥaḏ) from my lord" — the Niphal of kāḥaḏ (to hide/conceal) expresses the Egyptians' transparency in admitting complete destitution. The "we cannot hide" formula is a confession of total dependence. kî ʾim-gĕwiyyātēnû wĕʾadmātēnû — "except our bodies and our land" — gĕwiyyāh (body, frame, physical person — also used for the body of the ark in Ezek 1:11) represents the only assets remaining after money and livestock are gone.
19: qĕnēh ʾōtānû wĕʾet-ʾadmātēnû baléḥem — "buy us and our land for bread" — the Egyptians' proposal to sell themselves and their land is the final stage of the asset-transfer. wĕnihyeh ʾănaḥnû wĕʾadmātēnû ʿăḇāḏîm lĕparʿōh — "and we with our land will be servants to Pharaoh" — the verb hāyāh (be) in the cohortative (nĕhyeh) expresses the people's willing self-offering: "let us be servants." The economic enserfment of Egypt (distinct from Israel's later compelled slavery) is the result of the famine; the Israelites in Goshen are explicitly protected from this process (v27).
20: wayyiqen yôsēp ʾet-kol-ʾadmat miṣrayim lĕparʿōh — Joseph "acquired/bought" (qānāh, the same verb as "Creator/Possessor of heaven and earth" in Gen 14:19) all the land of Egypt for Pharaoh. The land-purchase by the state is the economic climax: all private landholding (except the priests', v22) is absorbed by Pharaoh. kî-ḥāzaq ʿălêhem hārāʿāḇ — "because the famine was too severe for them" (ḥāzaq, to be strong/pressing — the same word for the hardening of Pharaoh's heart in Exodus).
21: wĕʾet-hāʿām heʿĕḇîr ʾōtô lĕʿārîm — "and the people he moved to cities" (or: "he reduced the people to servitude"). The Hebrew text is ambiguous: heʿĕḇîr (Hiphil of ʿāḇar, to cause to pass/move) can mean "he moved them" (geographically, from country to cities for food distribution) or, with a different reading, "he enslaved them." The Samaritan Pentateuch and LXX read differently; most modern translations follow the MT's "moved to cities" — consistent with the food-distribution logistics of a centralized famine-response.
22: raq ʾadmat hakkōhănîm lōʾ qānāh — "only the land of the priests he did not buy." The exemption of priestly land is consistent with Egyptian practice: temple estates were traditionally exempt from royal taxation and appropriation in Egypt. The reason given — kî ḥōq lakkōhănîm mēʾēt parʿōh — "because there was a fixed allotment for the priests from Pharaoh" (ḥōq, statute/fixed portion — the same word as the legal statute in v26 and as the Mosaic "statutes" of the law). The Egyptian priestly class had a separate supply-chain from the royal granaries.
23: hinnēh qānîtî ʾetkem hayyôm wĕʾet-ʾadmatkĕm lĕparʿōh — "now I have bought you and your land today for Pharaoh." The hinnēh (behold) draws attention to the formal declaration of the economic transaction; the perfect tense (qānîtî, I have acquired) marks the completed action. hēʾ-lākem zera — "here is seed for you" — the provision of seed following the acquisition establishes the sharecropping arrangement: the land belongs to Pharaoh; the people will work it; the harvest is divided.
24: ûhāyāh baṭṭĕḇûʾōt ûnĕtattem ḥămîšît lĕparʿōh — "and when the harvest comes in, give a fifth (ḥōmeš) to Pharaoh." The ḥōmeš (fifth, 20%) is the tax rate established as Joseph's land-policy. The remaining four-fifths remain with the farmers for seed, food, and household needs. The 20% agricultural tax is consistent with known ANE administrative practices (Mesopotamian temple-estate taxation often ran 10-20%); the Egyptian system formalized here runs as a law "still in force today" (v26), embedding the Josephine policy in Egyptian agricultural history.
25: hĕḥĕyîtānû — "you have saved our lives" — the Hiphil perfect of ḥāyāh (to live/cause to live): Joseph "caused us to live." The same verb-form appears in the Rahab narrative (Josh 2:13: "you will spare our lives"), the widow of Zarephath (1 Kgs 17:13: "make food for me first"), and throughout the Joseph cycle (45:7: "to preserve a remnant on earth"). nimṣāʾ-ḥēn bĕʿênê ʾăḏōnî — "may we find favor (ḥēn) in the eyes of my lord" — the grace-formula of patron-client relationship.
26: wayyāśem ʾōtāh yôsēp lĕḥōq — "Joseph established it as a statute (ḥōq)." The word ḥōq (fixed ordinance/statute) is the same term used for Pharaoh's allotment to the priests (v22) and the Mosaic law-statutes (ḥuqqôt). The "law (ḥōq) concerning land... still in force today" — the narrative note grounds the Josephine tax policy in the reader's present: a known Egyptian agricultural practice is traced to Joseph's administration. ʿaḏ hayyôm hazzeh — "until this day/to this day" is the standard formula for an etiology whose effect persists into the narrator's own time.
27: wayyiprû wayyirbu mĕʾōḏ — "they were fruitful and increased greatly" — the creation-blessing formula (pārāh ûrāḇāh, be fruitful and multiply) of Gen 1:28, applied to the covenant-people multiplying in Egypt. The same formula will open Exodus (Exod 1:7: "the Israelites were exceedingly fruitful; they multiplied greatly"), connecting the Goshen-flourishing of Genesis 47 to the Egypt-multiplication crisis of Exodus 1. The covenant-promise of innumerable offspring (13:16; 15:5) is being fulfilled in Egypt — precisely through the famine that drove them there.
28: šĕḇaʿ ʿeśrēh šānāh — "seventeen years" in Egypt — Jacob entered Egypt at 130 (v9) and died at 147. The seventeen years mirrors the number of years Joseph spent in Jacob's household before the brothers sold him: Joseph was 17 when sold (37:2), seventeen years with his father; Jacob lived seventeen years with Joseph in Egypt. The numerical mirror (17 + 17) is a deliberate structural balance in the Joseph narrative's architecture.
29: śîm-nāʾ yāḏĕkā taḥat yĕrēkî — "put your hand under my thigh" — the oath-by-thigh gesture (also 24:2,9: Abraham's servant's oath to find Isaac's wife). The yārēk (thigh/loin) oath places the sworn commitment in proximity to the procreative seat of the family's continuity: swearing by the loins is swearing by everything the future holds. ḥesed weʾĕmet — the covenant word-pair of loyal-love (ḥesed) and faithfulness/truth (ʾĕmet), appearing together as the summary of covenant-loyalty (Exod 34:6; Ps 86:15; Prov 3:3; John 1:14: "full of grace and truth").
30: wĕšāḵaḇtî ʿim-ʾăḇōtāy — "when I rest with my fathers" — the šākaḇ ʿim ʾăḇōtāyw (lie down with one's fathers) idiom is the standard OT death-formula (used throughout Kings and Chronicles for royal deaths). ûnĕśāʾtanî miṣrayim — "carry me up from Egypt" — the Qal of nāśāʾ (to lift/carry/bear) — the same verb for bearing the Ark (Num 4:15,19-20) and for the cloud bearing Israel (Isa 63:9). Jacob's body is to be borne back like the covenant-object it is, returning to the land of promise where the covenant-dead belong.
31: wayyištaḥû yiśrāʾēl ʿal-rōʾš hammiṭṭāh — "Israel worshiped leaning on the top of his miṭṭāh." The consonantal Hebrew מטה is ambiguous: voweled as miṭṭāh (bed, MT), or as maṭṭēh (staff/rod, as the LXX reads: ἐπὶ τὸ ἄκρον τῆς ῥάβδου αὐτοῦ). The LXX/Heb 11:21 reading (staff) has Jacob worshiping as he leans on his pilgrim's staff — a powerful image of faith in extremis. The MT reading (bed) has him leaning on the headboard of his deathbed as he bows in worship. The two readings represent different vowel-pointing traditions of the same consonants; Heb 11:21 cites the LXX version as Jacob's act of faith at the end of his life.
